customer_addr:
505 KING AVENUE ROOM NO. 11-8-070
COLUMBUS, OH 43201

citations.narrative:
The facility self- reported an incident where, on March 28, 2026, one non-human primate (a Cynomolgus macaque) was
able to exit its enclosure by pushing out the waste pan and maneuvering through that space. The waste pans are meant
to be removeable to facilitate cleaning. The non-human primate was unharmed and was returned to its enclosure without
further incident. The staff were able to confirm that the non-human primate left its primary enclosure due no locking
mechanisms present on the catch pan. The locking mechanism is a secondary means used by the facility to ensure that
the pan could not be removed by the non-human primate after clicking the latch that is adhered to the rack by the rack
manufacturer.
Primary enclosures that do not securely contain the animal can lead to animal escapes, stress, or injuries. Animal
enclosures must be maintained so that they contain the non-human primates securely and prevent accidental opening of
the enclosure, including opening by the animal.
The facility had identified this issue prior to today's inspection. They have developed a system of color coded locks to
ensure the locks are properly utilized. Training of employees to this system has been implemented. Lock checks are now
required to be documented in electronic data records. Management also developed a rack preparation checklist to be
utilized by staff to ensure that racks are functioning as intended for non-human primates. Lastly, management evaluated
their inventory of different housing racks for non-human primates for suitability of further use.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representatives.
n

narrative:
3.80(a)(2)(iii)  Repeat
   
Primary enclosures. 
The facility self- reported an incident where, on March 28, 2026, one non-human primate (a Cynomolgus macaque) was 
able to exit its enclosure by pushing out the waste pan and maneuvering through that space. The waste pans are meant 
to be removeable to facilitate cleaning. The non-human primate was unharmed and was returned to its enclosure without 
further incident. The staff were able to confirm that the non-human primate left its primary enclosure due no locking 
mechanisms present on the catch pan. The locking mechanism is a secondary means used by the facility to ensure that 
the pan could not be removed by the non-human primate after clicking the latch that is adhered to the rack by the rack 
manufacturer.  
 
Primary enclosures that do not securely contain the animal can lead to animal escapes, stress, or injuries. Animal 
enclosures must be maintained so that they contain the non-human primates securely and prevent accidental opening of 
the enclosure, including opening by the animal.  
 
The facility had identified this issue prior to today's inspection. They have developed a system of color coded locks to 
ensure the locks are properly utilized. Training of employees to this system has been implemented. Lock checks are now 
required to be documented in electronic data records. Management also developed a rack preparation checklist to be 
utilized by staff to ensure that racks are functioning as intended for non-human primates. Lastly, management evaluated 
their inventory of different housing racks for non-human primates for suitability of further use. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with the facility representatives.
